content.text:
*"From one man he made all the nations."*{Acts 17:26}{*"God made the integers; all else is the work of man."*}{Leopold Kronecker}

*"From the single element $1$, all integers are generated."*{â€” Mathematical folklore}

Among all groups, the cyclic groups are the simplest and most fundamental. 
They are generated by a single element, yet they appear throughout 
mathematics and model phenomena from clock arithmetic to the symmetries 
of a circle.

content.title: Cyclic Group
content.content:
A group $G$ is **cyclic** if there exists an 
element $g \in G$ such that $G = \langle g \rangle = \{g^n : n \in \Z\}$. 
The element $g$ is called a **generator** of $G$.

content.title: The fundamental cyclic groups
content.problem:
- $(\Z, +)$ is cyclic with generator $1$ (or $-1$). Every integer 
 is a multiple of $1$.

- $(\Z_n, +)$ is cyclic with generator $1$. Every element is a 
 multiple of $1$ modulo $n$.

content.title: Structure of Cyclic Groups
content.content:
Every cyclic group is isomorphic to either $\Z$ (if infinite) or 
$\Z_n$ (if of finite order $n$).
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
Let $G = \langle g \rangle$.

**Case 1:** If $|g| = \infty$, then $g^i = g^j$ implies $i = j$ 
(otherwise $g^{i-j} = e$ with $i - j \neq 0$). The map $\phi: \Z \to G$ 
defined by $\phi(n) = g^n$ is a bijective homomorphism.

**Case 2:** If $|g| = n$, then $g^i = g^j$ if and only if 
$n \mid (i - j)$, i.e., $i \equiv j \pmod{n}$. The map 
$\phi: \Z_n \to G$ defined by $\phi(k) = g^k$ is a bijective homomorphism.

content.text:
Which elements generate $\Z_n$? This is a beautiful number-theoretic 
question with a clean answer.

content.title: Generators of $\Z_n$
content.content: The element $k \in \Z_n$ generates $\Z_n$ if and only if $\gcd(k, n) = 1$.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
By Theorem [thm:order-divides], $|k| = n / \gcd(k, n)$.

If $\gcd(k, n) = 1$, then $|k| = n$, so $\langle k \rangle$ has $n$ 
elements, hence $\langle k \rangle = \Z_n$.

If $\gcd(k, n) = d > 1$, then $|k| = n/d < n$, so $\langle k \rangle 
\neq \Z_n$.

The number of generators of $\Z_n$ equals $\phi(n)$, Euler's totient 
function.
content.label: Corollary

content.title: Generators of $\Z_{12}$
content.problem:
The generators of $\Z_{12}$ are those $k$ with $\gcd(k, 12) = 1$:
\[
 \{1, 5, 7, 11\}
\]
Thus $\phi(12) = 4$.

content.content:
Let $G = \langle g \rangle$ and let $a, b \in G$. Then $a = g^m$ and 
$b = g^n$ for some integers $m, n$. Thus:
\[
 ab = g^m g^n = g^{m+n} = g^{n+m} = g^n g^m = ba
\]

content.title: Subgroups of Cyclic Groups
content.content: Every subgroup of a cyclic group is cyclic.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
Let $G = \langle g \rangle$ and let $H \leq G$. If $H = \{e\}$, then 
$H = \langle e \rangle$ is cyclic.

Otherwise, $H$ contains some $g^k$ with $k \neq 0$. Let $m$ be the 
smallest positive integer such that $g^m \in H$. We claim $H = 
\langle g^m \rangle$.

Clearly $\langle g^m \rangle \subseteq H$. For the reverse, let 
$g^k \in H$. By the division algorithm, $k = qm + r$ with $0 \leq r < m$. 
Then $g^r = g^k (g^m)^{-q} \in H$. Since $m$ is minimal and $r < m$, we 
must have $r = 0$. Thus $g^k = (g^m)^q \in \langle g^m \rangle$.

items:
Prove that if $G$ is cyclic of order $n$ and $d \mid n$, then 
 $G$ has exactly one subgroup of order $d$.

The group $U(n)$ is sometimes cyclic. Determine whether $U(8)$, 
 $U(9)$, and $U(10)$ are cyclic, and find generators for those 
 that are.
Prove that every group of prime order is cyclic.

Let $G = \langle g \rangle$ be cyclic of order $n$. Prove that 
 $g^k$ is a generator of $G$ if and only if $\gcd(k, n) = 1$.

marginNotes.text:
And God said, "Let there be light," and 
there was light.

marginNotes.author: Leonhard Euler
marginNotes.text:
Mathematicians have tried in vain to this 
day to discover some order in the sequence of prime numbers.
